sentences.text: Take the cellular phone and just--
I don't know but take it off to the route.
I was born in a town in the eastern part of France-- not very far from the German border, which is called Alsace, where my father was born, and myself, and my brother.
And then at a very, very young age-- I wasn't even three when we left there.
And then the first World War broke out, and then we went to Switzerland.
I was born in 1911, on October 22nd-- which makes me 84 years old.
Not very pleasant to remember that.
[LAUGHS]
Three years old.
I was born in '11, and it broke out in 1914.
And all during the war, from 1914 to 1918, I was in Switzerland with my parents and my brother.
Well, you still have memory.
I was a small child, but I still have memory.
I remember MY-- my father was a big wheel in the-- in the-- whatever you call-- FBI, the French FBI in Switzerland.
And there were office of the FBI for every country in Switzerland.
And in old days, they never wanted-- they never wanted any of them and they tried to arrest all of them.
But if they could catch them, of course, they were arrested.
But Switzerland being a neutral country, they didn't know any-- didn't want any part of pro or con.
But we lived there during the entire part of the war, and got back to Paris in 1919.
Oh, sure.
I went to school.
I learned all kinds of things that the French student never learned.
I learned how to knit, how to crochet.
I know-- I learned how to embroid-- and I learned an awful lot better-- better in every subject we were taught.
Arithmetic and spelling, and all of that-- I was much, much ahead of the French children when I got back to school in Paris.
I was eight years old.
Well, the life-- the life of a French Jewish girl, I think I'm typical.
I mean, we are French since the 17th century, and didn't have very much to do with the people who immigrated from-- from Eastern Europe.
And all we knew was about our life and I can't say that I ever-- ever was exposed to anti-Semitism, or-- never in my time and never-- it never happened to me.
But I was saying before that, since the Dreyfus affair, I think that my-- my youth happened to be following the Dreyfus affair.
And the French were pretty ashamed of what happened during Dreyfus, and I think that we benefited from that.
And that might be one reason why I never-- I never knew any anti-Semitism.
Oh, my God-- I hope everybody remembers that.
This was Captain Dreyfus who was denounced as being a spy in the French army, and was denounced by a man by the name of Esterhazy.
And he was going to be, I expect, killed or in prison for life.

And Emile Zola, the famous writer, had enough courage to write something very, very well-known called, where he is accusing the French government and the military for having-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for having tried to-- to convict a man who wasn't-- who was innocent.
And he was finally-- he was finally rehabilitated.
And it was something that the French-- the French felt quite ashamed about it, because it involved the whole French people.
I mean, everybody was anti-Dreyfus.
No, I don't say-- I don't say I felt a feeling of shame.
I say that this might be one of the reason why we were never-- we were never treated badly, because we were Jewish.
I mean, I was never called a dirty Jew.
I never-- anything like this happen to me.
And being French for so many generations, we were profoundly French, and I lived a life of all the French girls.
And I went to the scouts for years and years, and I-- I used to do fencing, and I had everything like everybody else-- piano lesson, dancing lesson, and everything else.

And it was a really normal kind of a life-- not prepared at all for anything drastic happening, having no-- no experience whatsoever until the first refugees came-- started to come from Germany and from Austria, were telling impossible stories to believe.
And the only answer we had was, oh, this is OK.
It's in Germany and in Austria.
It's never going to be in France.
Oh, my God-- when I was married already.
No-- I mean, the first refugees came after 1933, and Hitler started-- I mean in 1933, then '34, '35, '36.
I mean, people who could get out tried to get out.
And so we heard-- we heard impossible stories that were not easy to believe.
And we always had that same reaction-- it can happen to them, it can never happen to us.
When I was growing up, I had a brother and when I was eight and a half years old I got a sister.
So I have a brother and a sister, and that's it-- and my parents.
My father worked, yes.
Well, my father was in a shoe business, and he was that all his life.
And involved in a lot of things, in a lot of different-- different organization.
He was mainly involved with a very famous shoe concern called Bata, which is from Czechoslovakia.
And he-- he introduced it in Western Europe.
And he finally he finally opened a big factory in eastern France, and employed 2,000 workers, and manufacturing in France instead of-- instead of importing from Czechoslovakia.
No.
My father was a fine champion of fencing.
He was a Vice President of the Fencing Federation of France-- and fencing was always the big thing in my household.
My brother did fencing, I did fencing, and we sometimes did it together.
And we were very much involved in that, because my father was very involved.
But there was no more-- there was no more activity, illegal activity, going on, I mean, when everything was normal.
After all, life was normal until the war broke out in 1939.
Well, my husband's grandfather was chief rabbi of Paris, so we had pretty good exposure.
And I was the first-- I was the very first student at the reform temple that was opened when I was about 12.
And I was the first student to attend this school.
And we only had one reform temple in Paris and it was never recognized as legal by the-- by the organization-- which is called the Consistoire.

And the Consistoire is the-- the-- well, that's-- it's an organization that all the temple belonged to, and they're the ones who nominate rabbis for different city in France, and-- and for Paris, of course.
And every temple belongs, every synagogue belonged, but the reform temple never did.
They never accepted it, and it was never considered legal.
Yeah.
It had the name of the-- of the street in which it was.
It was Temple Copernic, because the street was Copernic.
It had a big, enormous other name.
I mean, the Union Liberale Israelite.
I mean, this is very hard for anyone to understand.
Yes.
Louis-Germain Levy.
And my teacher-- my teacher who had a profound influence on me, was a certain Aime Palliere who is very well-known.
Whoever has a-- a very good knowledge of Jewish people and Jewish well-known people, know who he was.
He was a priest.
He was a priest by formation, priest by-- by dedication until-- until he suddenly had an opening in the Jewish religion.
And for him, there was finally nothing else.
And so he became-- he became assistant rabbi in my temple, and he was my teacher Sunday school.
And I learned more from him than I would ever have learned from anybody else.
And it was very important to me, very important.
In school?
I went up to the Sorbonne.
Yes.
Two and a half years.
I studied philosophy and psychology.
Well, I remember-- I mainly remember one year that I spent at the College de France, and the professor was Pierre Janet-- a very, very famous philosopher.
And my Sorbonne years-- well, were just school years.
I mean, nothing special.
After that, I was-- I mean, what I really did the most was to go into nursing, and I became a Red Cross nurse.
Yes.
I was taking nursing lessons.
I mean, I was-- the French Red Cross is entirely different from the Red Cross all over the world.
I mean, we are three years or four years of training from first aid to RN.
And if you take the four years, you become an RN, but you don't-- you don't get paid.
I mean, it's not-- you-- you have the same title, and you have the same background as any registered nurse, but you don't get paid.
You can-- you have to sign a paper that you will be in a war, and that you will be-- you will be serving in the war.
You can be drafted as soon as the war breaks out.
But when you have children, you can get out of the draft.
20-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19 Or 20.
Well, I learned one year in first aid, and the second year for first-year-- first-year nursing, and second-year nursing, and third-year nursing.
I mean, regular nursing courses.
I mean, I was ready-- when the world war broke out I was ready to be taken going and go on the Maginot Line.
I never met my husband.
He was a cousin of mine.
We were related.
Oh, it-- it happened.
It happened, but we were practically brought up together, so that I mean-- I never had a new family when I married my husband.
Did you and your husband decide to marry to each other, or was it something that was somehow decided in the family?
No, not.
We were not Arabs.
Only the Arabs are meant to marry the man that-- that they're given to them.
And it usually happen when they're born.
One family promised a child of another family that they will marry.
And they usually don't even know each other before the day of the wedding.
That's not happen in France.
In France, you make your own decision and you do what you want.
No, the parents are never very happy when you marry a cousin.
They're always afraid that it might have a repercussion on the children.
It's not good to be married to somebody--
Hm?
No, they didn't.
Again, they weren't especially happy about it.
I mean-- on either side, I think, they would have liked each one of us to go-- to be part of a new family.
But we didn't.
20-- When I got married?
[SIGHS] 23.
And my husband was 25.
I was working-- I was-- I wasn't working.
I mean, you did-- you could, if you wanted to, you could go to a hospital and take a morning, for instance, and be there.
You could do what you want.
I mean, we are completely free to decide what you wanted.
The only thing that you were bound to do was to go in a war if the war breaks out.
And so during the war, I didn't go on the front line first.
I went to the Maginot Line for a while.
But I mainly tried to form nurses to send to the front line.
And as the war was not a real war-- it was what they called a phony war-- we never had really a war that was bad enough to have wounded soldier or to have bad cases.
But at the end of that phony war, after eight months, I had had formed about 25 nurses, but I couldn't send them anywhere, because there was no need them.
And that was finally June, 1940, and that was the time when the-- the German went around the Maginot Line-- never used it, and went through Belgium and crossed into France.
And France was done in seven days.
I was living-- that summer of 1939, I was living at the seashore.
My parents had taken a house on the seashore.
I was there with my children and stayed there, and that's where I-- I trained these nurses.
And then when everybody had to go, and we had an exit or something through France, when the entire French people went down south to try to avoid the Germans who were coming down from the north.

And they were-- they were-- they were filling the roads in such a way that-- that the older tanks and the French army could not even try to go and to defend themselves, because the whole population of France was-- was closing the roads.
But at that time, I was assigned to a certain town where I was supposed to meet the doctors and a surgeon I had been with before when I was on the Maginot Line.
And we were supposed to take care of behind the scene of the wounded soldiers who would be coming down, who would be sent down in ambulance.
Well, there wasn't very much wounded, but I was all by myself.
My-- my ambulance-- we called that an ambulance, we put the people in-- the nurse, the surgeon, the doctors.
My ambulance was stuck behind the line that the German made of France.
They separated France in two.
And I was alone, and had 500 wounded soldiers in a school that had transformed into a hospital, and was alone for four days with them.

My husband was taken in the north of France when the-- the 10th of June, when the Germans invaded from Belgium through-- through the northern part of France, and he was part of one battalion who fell in their hands.
And life was so terrible at that time that all the top-- the top rank officers had left.
They just disappeared-- to and pick up their wife and family and save them.
And my husband remained as a lieutenant.
He was in charge of his whole battalion and was told by the French radio to leave their arm, leave all your armament intact and flee.
But he never fled, never left his armament intact.
They took-- they used everything they could until they were surrounded and they could do no more.
Well, it's a very-- it's a really easy recollection, because I was married in January 1934.
We left for-- for Switzerland.
We were on our honeymoon for about six weeks, and we got back to Paris.
And during-- during the time that we had been gone, there had been terrible strikes in France, and there was a terrible- a terrible movement from the workers against-- against their employer.
And what we found in coming back was a tremendous turmoil in France.

And as we went from 1934 to 1939, this is my first five years of married life, where we never had a life because Leon Blum became the first Jewish prime minister, and that never happened in France before.
And he was a socialist, so that France became together a socialist and also-- and also led by a Jew.
And this didn't create any good feeding.
I mean, there was a lot of riots and a lot of-- a lot of turmoil.
And in 1938, Chamberlain went to Germany and made a pact with Hitler.
And when he came back from-- from it, from England-- from Germany to England-- he picked up the radio and said to the world, I have avoided war for the last-- for the end of a century.
There will never be a war and we've got-- Hitler and I dealt together, and we are avoiding a war for the rest of the century.
And eight months later, August 1939, the war broke out.
So these were my first five years of married life.
Then the next five years it was war-- so my husband was fighting and fighting in the French resistance and--
Terrible.
It was very, very bad.
I mean, everybody was saying there's not-- no time to get children, you shouldn't have children in a time like this.
We still all said we want children.
How much do we wait, and when do we do?
We're waiting.
And so I had my daughter who was born in '35, and my second daughter was born in '39-- three months, four months before the war broke out.
And from that time on, of course, our life was completely-- disintegrated completely.
I was at that-- at a seashore.
My husband was mobilized.
He was with his-- with his army corps, and went into the war.
I mean, he went on the Maginot Line like-- like most of them did.
And this line was built in order to stop the German from ever invading France, but they turned around.
They didn't have-- they didn't have to go through it.
They turned around and went into Belgium way up in the frontier, on the northern frontier and invade France from the north instead of doing it from the east.
Why he was taken prisoner?
No, I said he was taken prisoner--
Yeah, and without my knowledge, because in being a Red Cross nurse I was entitled certain details of what was going on that maybe just the-- the private people didn't get.
But I request all kinds of information to all kinds of places, and never got it.
And finally, one day I received a postcard.
I didn't know if he hadn't been killed.
I mean, I had no idea he was prisoner.
And finally I received a postcard, and the postcard was all printed.
All you could do in put yes or no.
So they'd say, for instance, I'm sick?
And said, no.
I am in good health?
It says, yes.
OK, that's all you could do, say yes or no.
And on that postcard it said, I'm a prisoner of war, yes or no.
Oh, sure.
Some time in the summer.
Sometime in-- I was where I was a nurse.
I was-- I was still in my hospital.
And I had a nurse for my children, and they were-- they were staying in a hotel there in that town.
And they-- they lived there with my nurse, and I lived in a hospital.
Well, and something very strange happened, because I had lived in that same town before-- two or three years before that-- and I knew quite a few people.
I remember a lot of people.

And one of my friend was at the railroad station every night, and she was distributing hot drinks and cookies, or whatever we had, to the soldiers who were in the train coming through that town going further down south.
And one-- one night, in the middle of the night, she saw somebody jump out of the railroad car and she said, oh my gosh-- that's Paulette's husband.
And so she went immediately to talk to him, and she said, oh, I feel so terrible because I saw Paulette a few days ago, and I know that she had all the papers and passport to go to Morocco.
And she was going to join up there and see Morocco-- I don't think she's here.
I think she left.
And he had escaped for the fourth time.
He tried three time before.
He was picked up again.
For the fourth time, he escaped the prisoner of war camp, and he walked from Germany to France.
And the peasants on the way gave him-- one gave him shirt, another one give him a pair of pants.
And-- I mean, and he didn't have his uniform-- the prisoner uniform anymore.
And so he walked and walked until he reached the border of France, and there he took a train and they accept-- all those of course in the trains, they didn't have to have any money to put ticket.
And when he came there, and he heard that I had-- that I wasn't there, can imagine how he felt.
And that night, I happened to be in a hotel with my children-- I wasn't in a hospital.
And at 4 o'clock in the morning, somebody knocked the door.
You know, when you survive all that, I mean, you could survive anything.
Since what?
From the time the war broke out.
From August 1939.
This was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August or September, '40.
Nothing.
I was sure he had been killed.

Well, I mean there was always hope and with as long as you never got any official-- any official response-- I mean, to any other card that you send and any of the mail that you send through the Red Cross, they never got a response.
And so, until that one card, that postcard came, I didn't know what happened to him.
I didn't know whether he was prisoner or he had been killed.
That was all until he suddenly came in.
Well, I mean-- I remember.
You can imagine what happened to-- to me and my children.
I mean, we-- we couldn't believe it.
We couldn't-- we never believed what would happen.
We never could tell.
And the minute-- the minute he came back, I mean, the only thing he had in mind was to be in the resistance.
And that's all-- that's all that count for him, and he had to fight.
And we could have come to America, because my parents in the meantime came to America in 1941, and we could have come.
But my husband always believed that being Jewish and-- and belonging to France, and being a French patriot, you don't flee to America.
It was to fight for your country.
No, then we settled.
We settled in another place.
We didn't stay where I was with the hospital.
And we settled in another place, and he almost immediately started to-- to work for the resistance.
And I think I told you a very interesting story that he was-- at that time he was managing a very big five and ten cents store.
When he got into the attic of that store, he found tons and tons of dry beans and sugar and green beans and yellow beans-- and tons of it.
And this was absolutely-- restricted.
There was no ration card, or no coupon that gave us the right to have any of that food.
So that food was simply deteriorating in the attic of that store, and nobody could benefit.
So he put an ad in a paper, and announced to the population that as of Monday 8 o'clock in the morning, he would be distributing anything that he found in the attic.
So at 8 o'clock in the morning, the whole population showed up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in front of that store, before it even opened.
And then everybody left with a big package of whatever was there and was-- could be distributed to entire population.
I mean, until the last one, everything was given away.
And about half an hour later, the Gestapo came and picked him up and put him in a fortress.
